fact: August 31 is the very last day of summer's final month, making it like the grand finale of a three-month summer party!
In the Northern Hemisphere, many trees start dropping their first yellow leaves around this time, like nature is testing out its fall costume.
Hummingbirds in North America are frantically stuffing themselves with nectar right now, doubling their body weight to prepare for their epic journey south.
The Sun is racing toward the autumn equinox, losing about 2-3 minutes of daylight every single day in most places.
August 31 falls smack in the middle of Virgo season, when this perfectionist zodiac sign is supposedly making everyone extra organized and picky.
Monarch butterflies are gathering in massive orange clouds across North America, getting ready for their mind-blowing 3,000-mile migration to Mexico.
In many places, morning dew starts getting heavier around this date because cooler nights meet still-warm days, creating perfect droplet conditions.
Crickets chirp faster or slower based on temperature, and you can actually calculate the exact temperature by counting their chirps on August 31 nights!
This date sits exactly 23 days before the autumn equinox, when day and night will be perfectly equal lengths everywhere on Earth.
In the Southern Hemisphere, August 31 marks the final day of winter, so spring flowers are already starting to poke through the soil.
Many apple varieties in northern climates reach peak ripeness right around now, which is why apple picking season kicks into high gear in early September.
The angle of sunlight hitting Earth on August 31 creates those golden, honey-colored sunsets that photographers go absolutely crazy for.
